1: vaessa et einai vaere vehineh ish uveyadoh chevel middah: 'And I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, a man with a measuring line in his hand!' The third vision: the man with the measuring line (ch2 in Hebrew versification; note that English ch2 = Hebrew ch2:1-13). The measuring line (chevel middah, H4060) is the surveyor's tool for planning construction. The man is going to measure Jerusalem — prepare for building. This contrasts with Lam 2:8 (YHWH stretched the line to destroy): now the line signals restoration, not destruction.
2: vaomar ana ata holekh vayomer elai lmad et Yerushalayim lir'ot mah rachbah umah arkah: 'And I said, Where are you going? And he said to me, To measure Jerusalem, to see what is its width and what is its length.' The city survey for future building. The fact that Jerusalem will be measured implies it will be rebuilt — and the following oracles (vv4-5) reveal that the rebuilt Jerusalem will be so large it cannot be walled: a city without walls, because YHWH himself will be its wall of fire.
3: vehineh hamalakh haddover bi yotze umalakh acher yotze liqrato: 'And behold, the angel who talked with me came forward, and another angel came forward to meet him.' Two angels — the interpretive angel and another — engage in dialogue. The second angel brings urgent information: the measuring is interrupted by a message too important to wait. The structure creates expectation: the normal procedure (measuring, survey) is suspended because YHWH has a word to speak about Jerusalem's future that transcends normal building plans.
4: vayomer elav rutz daber el hana'ar hallaz leimor perazot teishev Yerushalayim merov adam uvehemah betokhah: 'And said to him, Run, say to that young man: Jerusalem shall be inhabited as villages without walls, because of the multitude of people and livestock in it.' Perazot (H6519 — open towns, unwalled villages) — not the embarrassment of an undefended city but the blessing of a population so large it cannot be contained within any wall. 'Villages without walls' is a prosperity image: settled, peaceful farming communities that have no need of defensive walls because YHWH is the defense. The multitude of people and livestock echoes the patriarchal blessing promises.
5: va'ani ehyeh lah neum YHWH chomatei esh saviv ulechavod ehyeh betokhah: 'And I will be to her a wall of fire all around, declares the LORD, and I will be the glory in her midst.' Two divine promises: (1) chomatei esh (wall of fire) — YHWH himself as the city's protection, beyond any physical fortification. Fire-as-divine-presence echoes the pillar of fire in the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22; 14:24). (2) leckhavod ehyeh betokhah (I will be the glory in her midst) — the Shekinah restored. The glory that departed from Jerusalem before the exile (Ezek 10-11) will return. The two promises together — external protection and internal divine presence — describe the eschatological city.
6: hoi hoi venusu me'eretz tzafon neum YHWH ki ke'arba ruchot hashamayim pirastiy etchem neum YHWH: 'Up! Up! Flee from the land of the north, declares the LORD. For I have spread you abroad as the four winds of the heavens, declares the LORD.' The urgent call to flee Babylon (eretz tzafon, the land of the north — Babylon is north of Israel by geography of travel) is addressed to the exiles who have not yet returned. YHWH scattered them (pirastiy, from H6566 = to spread, scatter) as the four winds scatter — now the reverse: they should gather themselves and flee back to the land.
7: hoi Tziyon himaletiy yoshevet bat Bavel: 'Up! Escape to Zion, you who dwell with the daughter of Babylon.' The second call to flee: 'daughter of Babylon' (bat Bavel) personifies the Babylonian empire as a city/woman. Those dwelling comfortably in Babylon's prosperity (its economic benefits for Jewish exiles are attested in the Murashu documents from Nippur) are called to choose Zion over Babylon. The NT parallel: Rev 18:4 ('Come out of her, my people') uses the same exodus-from-Babylon imagery for eschatological departure from worldly systems opposed to God.
8: ki koh amar YHWH tzvaot achar kavod shelachani el hagoyim hashollim etchem ki hannogea bakhem noge'a bevavat eino: 'For thus said the LORD of hosts, after his glory sent me to the nations who plundered you, for he who touches you touches the apple of his eye.' The phrase bavat eino (the apple/pupil of his eye, H892) — the most sensitive and vulnerable part of the eye, used metaphorically for that which YHWH most carefully guards. Israel/Jerusalem is the apple of YHWH's eye: to touch them is to touch YHWH himself at his most sensitive point. The motif of divine protection through radical identification with his people is the ground for the following judgment on the nations.
9: ki hineni menif et yadi aleihem vehayu shalal le'avdeihem vedatem ki YHWH tzvaot shelachani: 'Behold, I will shake my hand over them, and they shall become plunder for those who served them. Then you will know that the LORD of hosts has sent me.' The divine hand shaken over the oppressor-nations (the same gesture used for judgment in Isa 11:15; 19:16) will reverse the power dynamic: the plundered become plunderers; the servants of the nations become their masters. 'You will know that YHWH of hosts sent me' — this authenticating phrase is said by the divine messenger who is both sent by YHWH and himself divine (the Angel of the LORD), anticipating the NT's Sent One Christology.
10: roni veSimchi bat Tziyon ki hineni ba veshakhanti betokhech neum YHWH: 'Sing and rejoice, O daughter of Zion, for behold, I am coming and I will dwell in your midst, declares the LORD.' The call to joy (roni veSimchi — the same verbs as Zeph 3:14) is grounded in YHWH's promise to dwell (shakhanti, from shakhan = to tabernacle/dwell, H7931) in Jerusalem's midst. Shakhan is the root of the Hebrew Shekhinah — the divine indwelling presence. The NT's most direct application: John 1:14 (the Word became flesh and tabernacled/dwelt among us, using the Greek eskēnōsen which translates shakhan). YHWH's coming to dwell is the incarnation.
11: venil'vu goyim rabbim el YHWH bayom hahu vehayu li le'am veshakhanti vetokhech veyada'at ki YHWH tzvaot shelachani elayich: 'And many nations shall join themselves to the LORD in that day, and shall be my people. And I will dwell in your midst, and you shall know that the LORD of hosts has sent me to you.' The joining of nations (venil'vu goyim rabbim, from lavah = to join, H3867) to YHWH — becoming his people — is the eschatological expansion of the covenant community beyond ethnic Israel. 'Shall be my people' (vehayu li le'am) is covenant adoption language: the covenant formula ('I will be your God and you shall be my people') extended to Gentile nations. Paul's theology of Gentile inclusion (Rom 9:24-26; Eph 2:11-22) develops this vision.
12: venachal YHWH et Yehudah chelqo al admat haqodesh uvacher od biYerushalayim: 'And the LORD will inherit Judah as his portion in the holy land, and will again choose Jerusalem.' The theological claim is striking: YHWH 'inherits' (nachal, H5157 — to inherit, possess as a heritage) Judah as his portion. The usual direction of inheritance is from God to people (Ps 16:5: YHWH is my portion and my cup), but here the direction reverses: YHWH inherits his people and land as his own treasured possession. The holy land (admat haqodesh) — the only occurrence of this specific phrase in the OT, anticipating the NT's 'holy land' terminology.
13: has kol basar mippenei YHWH ki ne'or mime'on qodsho: 'Be silent, all flesh, before the LORD, for he has roused himself from his holy dwelling.' The universal call to silence (has, H2013 — the same hush-command as Hab 2:20; Zeph 1:7; Amos 6:10) before YHWH who is stirring from his holy dwelling. The divine rousing (ne'or mime'on qodsho) implies YHWH has been, as it were, inactive — and now he wakes for decisive action. The cosmic silence that precedes divine intervention: all flesh holds its breath before the moment of divine appearance. The NT uses the same silence imagery in Rev 8:1 (silence in heaven for half an hour before the trumpet judgments).
